# Thin film lithium niobate foundry
![Tfln Hero](https://images.ctfassets.net/i1dyhzbyi8ad/3VEpYmaq2n3MC8H7ijrHKb/d9913622ad5dd63658ae08db83418a7f/wafer_test-7.3.3.png)
# Thin film lithium niobate foundry
QCi built a first of its kind foundry devoted to processing thin film lithium niobate (TFLN) and other transition metal ferroelectrics in Tempe, Arizona. We closely work with our clients on conventional TFLN projects and also undertake highly custom engagements that leverage our extensive design, fabrication and characterization expertise.
Thin film lithium niobate is rapidly emerging as a key enabler for the next generation of telecom/datacom hardware supporting data rates of 1.6 Tbps, 3.2 Tbps and beyond. TFLN modulators operate at a low Vπ-length product, thereby facilitating low power consumption and reducing the device size. TFLN is also ideal for high-density heterogeneous integration and optical copackaging. Furthermore, nonlinear optical devices based on periodically poled lithium niobate (PPLN) will give rise to transformative technologies for quantum computing, remote sensing, and quantum imaging. 
To discuss the scope of your project and ways of collaborating with the QCi Foundry, please connect with us.
[](https://quantumcomputinginc.com/foundry/contact-us)
* * *
## What we make
The foundry offers both front and back end capabilities for photonic integrated circuits (PICs), including packaging for a 150 mm wafer line. We further support many of our customers throughout the entirety of their project offering design, simulation, and characterization services for TFLN devices. The most common devices fabricated at QCi are EOMs, phase modulators, ring resonators and filters, non-linear PPLN waveguides and microrings, and many more.
#### Passive devices (Micro rings)
#### Linear devices (EOMs)
#### Non-linear devices (PPLN)
* * *
## Enabling integrated photonics
Thin film lithium niobate, or TFLN, is a crystalline material with large optical nonlinearities. TFLN is at the forefront of miniaturizing optical systems and significantly extending the capabilities of bulk lithium niobate, which has been an industry standard for over 60 years as the active element in transducers. 
[](https://quantumcomputinginc.com/learn/research-and-publications/tfln-devices)
![Integrated Photonics 1](https://images.ctfassets.net/i1dyhzbyi8ad/PjnfrL9iTmq3NO2fUIcoX/898219ae269937863166abdff21dd319/left.jpg)
Using our proprietary etching methods, QCi optimized the TFLN etch processes and achieve an impressive 0.3 nm sidewall roughness.
![Integrated Photonics 2](https://images.ctfassets.net/i1dyhzbyi8ad/rfXeXwXoDCi9XX1TbcX8p/f10d1b8226ff69002762cd5846ba69b7/right.jpg)
QCi is actively developing a comprehensive library of TFLN components and PDKs. The image above is from an FDTD simulation of a microring resonator.
* * *
## The future of integrated photonics
Thin film lithium niobate (TFLN) has many benefits for both photonic and quantum technologies. Its unique optical properties allow for faster, more efficient control of light, which is essential in quantum computing and communication. By integrating TFLN into our own quantum technology, we are able to leverage these benefits to enhance the precision and performance of our systems.
[](https://quantumcomputinginc.com/learn/research-and-publications/thin-film-lithium-niobate-tfln)
#### High Density Encoding
![encoding v2](https://images.ctfassets.net/i1dyhzbyi8ad/6TtjhAPsp6djrZj3fRKcJA/f5e135c6284b8f9acb7b8b74a479a691/encoding.svg)
TFLN can extend the modulation bandwidth and efficiency of existing telecom infrastructure without extending the spectral range.It also enables 200 Gb+ per lane encoding resulting in high baud rates for 1.6 and 3.2 terabit devices.
#### Power Efficiency
![LowPower](https://images.ctfassets.net/i1dyhzbyi8ad/5qj0C9dIHD65mrdHUz18Y8/7a04281b9b221477ec1c1cef8fbd424f/power.svg)
TFLN offers a considerable advantage for energy efficiency (and greenhouse gas reduction) which stems from ultra-low insertion and propagation losses and low driving voltages (differential driving with CMOS allows sub-volt modulation). TFLN’s unprecedented modulation efficiency enables fast switching which consumes less than five femtojoules per bit.
#### Heterogeneous Integration
![Integration](https://images.ctfassets.net/i1dyhzbyi8ad/1gnYw7IaeEBHqrw8YdTyCD/0c183490bf5a57725d45558f37d5f87f/integration.svg)
TFLN is ideal for high density integration with CMOS via flip-chip bonding as well as other emerging bonding and packaging techniques. This capability extends the lifetime of pluggables and further enables copackaged optics. The ability to fabricate diverse linear, nonlinear and electro-optical components on the TFLN PIC, and the compatibility with CMOS devices, further enables integration with existing infrastructure.

class="MuiBox-root css-xb3nle"><h1 class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-h1 css-drc8au">Thin film lithium niobate foundry</h1><p class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-body1 css-1osaz8o">QCi built a first of its kind foundry devoted to processing thin film lithium niobate (TFLN) and other transition metal ferroelectrics in Tempe, Arizona. We closely work with our clients on conventional TFLN projects and also undertake highly custom engagements that leverage our extensive design, fabrication and characterization expertise.</p><p class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-body1 css-1osaz8o">Thin film lithium niobate is rapidly emerging as a key enabler for the next generation of telecom/datacom hardware supporting data rates of 1.6 Tbps, 3.2 Tbps and beyond. TFLN modulators operate at a low Vπ-length product, thereby facilitating low power consumption and reducing the device size. TFLN is also ideal for high-density heterogeneous integration and optical copackaging. Furthermore, nonlinear optical devices based on periodically poled lithium niobate (PPLN) will give rise to transformative technologies for quantum computing, remote sensing, and quantum imaging. </p><p class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-body1 css-1osaz8o">To discuss the scope of your project and ways of collaborating with the QCi Foundry, please connect with us.</p></div><div class="MuiCardActions-root MuiCardActions-spacing css-q2z7x"><a class="MuiButtonBase-root MuiButton-root MuiButton-contained MuiButton-containedSecondary MuiButton-sizeMedium MuiButton-containedSizeMedium MuiButton-colorSecondary MuiButton-disableElevation MuiButton-root MuiButton-contained MuiButton-containedSecondary MuiButton-sizeMedium MuiButton-containedSizeMedium MuiButton-colorSecondary MuiButton-disableElevation css-18lx8xx" tabindex="0" target="_blank" href="/foundry/contact-us"><span class="MuiButton-icon MuiButton-startIcon MuiButton-iconSizeMedium css-cveorv"><svg class="MuiSvgIcon-root MuiSvgIcon-fontSizeMedium css-q7mezt" focusable="false" aria-hidden="true" viewBox="0 0 18 18" width="18" height="18" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><path d="M7.27869 4.5L6.22119 5.5575L9.65619 9L6.22119 12.4425L7.27869 13.5L11.7787 9L7.27869 4.5Z"></path></svg></span>Connect with us</a></div></div></div><hr class="MuiDivider-root MuiDivider-fullWidth css-1oipzb5"><h2 class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-h2 css-rt5paw" id="what-we-make">What we make</h2><div class="MuiBox-root css-mx2j0q"><div class="MuiBox-root css-1k7nn9l"><p class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-body1 css-1osaz8o">The foundry offers both front and back end capabilities for photonic integrated circuits (PICs), including packaging for a 150 mm wafer line. We further support many of our customers throughout the entirety of their project offering design, simulation, and characterization services for TFLN devices. The most common devices fabricated at QCi are EOMs, phase modulators, ring resonators and filters, non-linear PPLN waveguides and microrings, and many more.</p></div><div class="MuiBox-root css-1u4ux2z"><div class="MuiPaper-root MuiPaper-elevation MuiPaper-rounded MuiPaper-elevation1 MuiCard-root css-oby4b1" style="--Paper-shadow:0px 2px 1px -1px rgba(0,0,0,0.2),0px 1px 1px 0px rgba(0,0,0,0.14),0px 1px 3px 0px rgba(0,0,0,0.12)"><div class="MuiBox-root css-s7kdc"><div class="MuiBox-root css-1qzwrnv"><h4 class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-h4 css-15ceqba" id="passive-devices-micro-rings">Passive devices (Micro rings)</h4></div></div></div><div class="MuiPaper-root MuiPaper-elevation MuiPaper-rounded MuiPaper-elevation1 MuiCard-root css-z6vdq3" style="--Paper-shadow:0px 2px 1px -1px rgba(0,0,0,0.2),0px 1px 1px 0px rgba(0,0,0,0.14),0px 1px 3px 0px rgba(0,0,0,0.12)"><div class="MuiBox-root css-s7kdc"><div class="MuiBox-root css-1qzwrnv"><h4 class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-h4 css-15ceqba" id="linear-devices-eoms">Linear devices (EOMs)</h4></div></div></div><div class="MuiPaper-root MuiPaper-elevation MuiPaper-rounded MuiPaper-elevation1 MuiCard-root css-c1qwy2" style="--Paper-shadow:0px 2px 1px -1px rgba(0,0,0,0.2),0px 1px 1px 0px rgba(0,0,0,0.14),0px 1px 3px 0px rgba(0,0,0,0.12)"><div class="MuiBox-root css-s7kdc"><div class="MuiBox-root css-1qzwrnv"><h4 class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-h4 css-15ceqba" id="non-linear-devices-ppln">Non-linear devices (PPLN)</h4></div></div></div></div></div><hr class="MuiDivider-root MuiDivider-fullWidth css-1oipzb5"><h2 class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-h2 css-rt5paw" id="enabling-integrated-photonics">Enabling integrated photonics</h2><div class="MuiBox-root css-5fzp6i"><div class="MuiBox-root css-0"><div class="MuiBox-root css-3lvcri"><p class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-body1 css-1osaz8o">Thin film lithium niobate, or TFLN, is a crystalline material with large optical nonlinearities. TFLN is at the forefront of miniaturizing optical systems and significantly extending the capabilities of bulk lithium niobate, which has been an industry standard for over 60 years as the active element in transducers. </p></div><div class="MuiCardActions-root MuiCardActions-spacing css-1n7ibuh"><a class="MuiButtonBase-root MuiButton-root MuiButton-contained MuiButton-containedSecondary The image above is from an FDTD simulation of a microring resonator.</p></div></div></div></div><hr class="MuiDivider-root MuiDivider-fullWidth css-1oipzb5"><h2 class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-h2 css-rt5paw" id="the-future-of-integrated-photonics">The future of integrated photonics</h2><div class="MuiBox-root css-5fzp6i"><div class="MuiBox-root css-0"><div class="MuiBox-root css-3lvcri"><p class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-body1 css-1osaz8o">Thin film lithium niobate (TFLN) has many benefits for both photonic and quantum technologies. Its unique optical properties allow for faster, more efficient control of light, which is essential in quantum computing and communication. By integrating TFLN into our own quantum technology, we are able to leverage these benefits to enhance the precision and performance of our systems.</p></div><div class="MuiCardActions-root class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-body1 css-1osaz8o">TFLN offers a considerable advantage for energy efficiency (and greenhouse gas reduction) which stems from ultra-low insertion and propagation losses and low driving voltages (differential driving with CMOS allows sub-volt modulation). TFLN’s unprecedented modulation efficiency enables fast switching which consumes less than five femtojoules per bit.</p></div></div><div class="MuiPaper-root MuiPaper-elevation MuiPaper-rounded MuiPaper-elevation1 MuiCard-root css-1kwu43w" style="--Paper-shadow:0px 2px 1px -1px rgba(0,0,0,0.2),0px 1px 1px 0px rgba(0,0,0,0.14),0px 1px 3px 0px rgba(0,0,0,0.12)"><div class="MuiBox-root css-1rhgfez"><h4 class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-h4 css-15ceqba" id="heterogeneous-integration">Heterogeneous Integration</h4><figure class="MuiBox-root css-h3csse"><img alt="Integration" loading="lazy" width="145" height="144" decoding="async" data-nimg="1" style="color:transparent;max-width:100%;height:fit-content" src="//images.ctfassets.net/i1dyhzbyi8ad/1gnYw7IaeEBHqrw8YdTyCD/0c183490bf5a57725d45558f37d5f87f/integration.svg"></figure><p class="MuiTypography-root MuiTypography-body1 css-1osaz8o">TFLN is ideal for high density integration with CMOS via flip-chip bonding as well as other emerging bonding and packaging techniques. This capability extends the lifetime of pluggables and further enables copackaged optics. The ability to fabricate diverse linear, nonlinear and electro-optical components on the TFLN PIC, and the compatibility with CMOS devices, further enables integration with existing
